questions.question: Explain the role of the interrupt controller (e.g., PIC and APIC) in the program interrupt cycle, including how it handles multiple interrupt requests, priority resolution, and provides the interrupt vector to the CPU.
keyPoints: Interrupt controller detects and prioritizes IRQ signals from peripherals.
It asserts the interrupt request line to the CPU and supplies the interrupt vector number.
In a PIC (e.g., 8259A) IRQ lines are hardwired; in an APIC (local+I/O) dynamic priority and routing are supported.
CPU uses the vector to index the IDT (x86) or vector table (ARM) to locate the ISR.

questions.question: Differentiate between hardware interrupts and software interrupts. Provide examples from x86 and ARM architectures and explain how each type is handled in the interrupt cycle.
keyPoints: Hardware interrupts are asynchronous signals generated by I/O devices (e.g., keyboard, timer).
Software interrupts are synchronous traps caused by executing a specific instruction (INT, SWI, syscall).
Hardware uses IRQ lines and the interrupt controller for vectoring; software uses the operand of the instruction as the vector.
Examples: IRQ0 (timer) and INT 0x80 (x86 syscall), IRQ (GIC) and SVC (ARM) for system calls.

questions.question: Describe the complete sequence of events in the program interrupt cycle, covering interrupt detection, CPU response, context saving, vector acquisition, and execution of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). Discuss the function of the interrupt controller (PIC/APIC) and the role of the Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) in x86 and the vector table in ARM. Also, analyze the factors that affect interrupt latency and how it impacts system performance.
keyPoints: Peripheral raises IRQ; interrupt controller (PIC/APIC) prioritizes and signals the CPU with the appropriate vector.
CPU finishes the current instruction, saves PC/PSW onto the stack, disables maskable interrupts, and fetches ISR address from IDT (x86) or vector table (ARM).
ISR executes, may be nested, handles I/O, sends End‑of‑Interrupt (EOI) to controller, then returns via IRET to restore state.
Interrupt latency = controller delay + pipeline flush + priority resolution + cache effects; higher latency degrades real‑time response.

questions.question: Explain the concepts of nested interrupts and priority levels in modern processors, focusing on the mechanisms provided by the x86 APIC and the ARM Generic Interrupt Controller (GIC). Discuss how the CPU preserves state for nested ISRs, the role of privilege levels, and the implications for real‑time system design.
keyPoints: Nested interrupts allow a higher‑priority interrupt to preempt a lower‑priority ISR; CPU must save additional state (extra stack frames) before executing the nested ISR.
x86 APIC uses Task Priority Register (TPR) and vector‑based priority; IDT entries include Descriptor Privilege Level (DPL) enabling privilege‑level transitions for nesting.
ARM GIC supports group priorities, priority masking, and ‘priority drop’; FIQ/IRQ modes bank registers, simplifying nested handling.
Proper nested interrupt handling prevents priority inversion and ensures deterministic latency for high‑priority real‑time tasks.
